stem: A group life scheme provides a lump-sum death benefit calculated as a multiple of the member's salary. Which of the following is the most common definition of 'salary' used for this purpose?
options: Basic salary only.
Basic salary plus average overtime.
Basic salary plus contractual bonuses.
Total remuneration package including all benefits.
explanation: The most common definition of salary used in group life schemes is basic salary only, though schemes can be set up to include other elements of remuneration.

stem: Under a group income protection scheme, the maximum insurable benefit is typically limited to a percentage of the member's pre-incapacity earnings. What is the usual maximum percentage?
options: 50% of earnings.
60% of earnings.
66.67% of earnings.
75% of earnings.
explanation: The usual maximum insurable benefit under a group income protection scheme is 75% of pre-incapacity earnings, though this includes the employer's National Insurance contributions on the benefit.

stem: A group income protection benefit is paid to the employer. The employer then pays the claimant through PAYE. This means the benefit is subject to
options: Income Tax only.
National Insurance contributions only.
both Income Tax and National Insurance contributions.
neither Income Tax nor National Insurance contributions.
explanation: When group income protection benefits are paid to the employer and then passed to the employee through PAYE, they are treated as earnings and are subject to both Income Tax and National Insurance contributions.

stem: Employer contributions to a registered group life scheme are generally treated for tax purposes as
options: a benefit in kind for the employee.
an allowable business expense for the employer.
a capital expenditure by the employer.
a non-deductible expense for the employer.
explanation: Employer contributions to a registered group life scheme are generally treated as an allowable business expense, deductible against corporation tax.

stem: Premiums paid by an employer for a group income protection scheme are treated for the employee as
options: a benefit in kind subject to Income Tax.
exempt from all taxation.
a deduction from the employee's personal allowance.
not a benefit in kind and therefore not taxable on the employee.
explanation: Employer-paid premiums for group income protection are not treated as a benefit in kind for the employee and therefore do not create a personal tax liability for the member.

stem: Under a group income protection scheme, what is the purpose of a 'limited payment term'?
options: To limit the period over which premiums are collected.
To restrict the maximum duration for which benefit is payable on any one claim.
To limit the number of claims a member can make.
To restrict the period during which new members can join.
explanation: A limited payment term restricts the maximum duration for which benefit is payable on any single claim, such as two years or five years, rather than paying to retirement age.

stem: The benefit escalation rate under a group income protection claim in payment is typically applied on
options: a simple interest basis.
a compound interest basis.
a stepped basis increasing every five years.
an index-linked basis tied to average earnings.
explanation: Benefit escalation under group income protection claims in payment is typically applied on a compound basis, meaning each year's increase is calculated on the previous year's escalated amount.

stem: A lump-sum death benefit paid from a registered group life scheme set up under a discretionary trust is
options: always subject to Income Tax.
always subject to Inheritance Tax.
normally free of both Income Tax and Inheritance Tax.
subject to Capital Gains Tax.
explanation: Lump-sum death benefits paid from a registered group life scheme under a discretionary trust are normally free of both Income Tax and Inheritance Tax, provided the benefit is within the lump sum and death benefit allowance.

stem: A group critical illness scheme typically pays a benefit that is
options: a regular income until the member recovers.
a single lump sum upon diagnosis of a specified condition.
an ongoing pension until retirement.
a reimbursement of medical expenses incurred.
explanation: Group critical illness provides a single lump-sum payment upon diagnosis of one of a defined list of serious medical conditions, subject to the member surviving any applicable survival period.

stem: An employer-paid group critical illness benefit is received by the employee. How is this benefit typically treated for tax purposes?
options: It is subject to Income Tax through PAYE.
It is subject to Capital Gains Tax.
It is received tax-free by the employee.
It is subject to National Insurance contributions only.
explanation: When group critical illness premiums are paid by the employer, the lump-sum benefit is typically treated as earnings and subject to Income Tax through PAYE when paid to the employee.

stem: Under a group income protection scheme, the 'own occupation' definition of incapacity means the member is considered incapacitated if they are unable to perform
options: any occupation for which they are suited by training or experience.
the material and substantial duties of their own specific job.
any paid employment whatsoever.
the duties of any occupation of similar status and earnings.
explanation: The 'own occupation' definition means the member is considered incapacitated if they are unable to perform the material and substantial duties of their own specific occupation.

stem: Which definition of incapacity under a group income protection scheme is generally considered the most generous to the claimant?
options: Any occupation.
Own occupation.
Suited occupation.
Activities of daily living.
explanation: The 'own occupation' definition is generally the most generous as it only requires the member to be unable to perform their specific job, rather than any job they could reasonably do.

stem: When an employee contributes towards the cost of a group income protection scheme, any benefit received will be
options: entirely tax-free.
partially tax-free in proportion to the employee's contribution.
fully subject to Income Tax.
subject to Capital Gains Tax.
explanation: Where an employee contributes towards the premium, the proportion of any benefit attributable to the employee's contributions is received tax-free, while the remainder is taxable.

stem: A group income protection scheme with a benefit basis of '50% of salary plus a fixed amount for employer National Insurance contributions' is designed to
options: reduce the employer's overall cost of the scheme.
ensure the employer is not out of pocket when paying the benefit through PAYE.
maximise the net benefit received by the claimant.
avoid the need for the benefit to be reported to HMRC.
explanation: Including an allowance for employer National Insurance contributions in the benefit basis ensures the employer is reimbursed for the NIC costs they incur when paying the benefit through PAYE.

stem: A dependant's pension paid from a group life scheme that is linked to a registered pension scheme is subject to
options: Income Tax but not National Insurance contributions.
National Insurance contributions but not Income Tax.
both Income Tax and National Insurance contributions.
neither Income Tax nor National Insurance contributions.
explanation: A dependant's pension is subject to Income Tax but not National Insurance contributions, as it is treated as pension income rather than earnings from employment.
